permissions.commentary: CC BY-SA 2.5 does not mention patents at all. Unlike CC 4.0, which expressly states that patent and trademark rights are not licensed, the 2.5 text neither grants nor reserves patent rights explicitly; the residual-rights clause (s-61) leaves any unstated patent claims with the rightsholder. Grey because a licensee has no express patent grant yet also has no express patent prohibition, and the scope of any implied patent grant under copyright-licensing doctrine is unsettled.

in the case of a Derivative Work, a credit identifying the use of the Work in the Derivative Work (e.g., 'French translation of the Work by Original Author,' or 'Screenplay based on original Work by Original Author').

conditions.commentary: CC BY-SA 2.5 does not use the explicit 'indicate if You modified' phrasing of 4.0, but §4(c) requires that every Derivative Work carry a credit identifying the nature of the use (e.g. 'translation of,' 'screenplay based on'), which functionally requires documenting the kind of change.

limitations.commentary: The 2.5 text addresses only Creative Commons' own trademark (the CC logo), not the licensee's or Licensor's trademarks. Unlike CC 4.0, there is no general 'patent and trademark rights are not licensed' statement, so the license is silent on third-party or Licensor trademarks in the Work itself.
